responses.strategic_rationale: Denmark’s digital-identity strategy for 2026–2029 emphasizes reduced dependence on a small set of foreign technology suppliers and stronger digital sovereignty. The five levers target (a) platform-neutral access to decouple trust from Apple/Google signals, (b) procurement neutrality to avert vendor lock-in, (c) credible demonstration of non-production fallbacks to de-risk adoption, (d) policy-coalition-building to shape procurement and EU alignment, and (e) rigorous continuity-risk management to preserve public access during disruption. The levers balance speed, cost, scope, and quality by layering governance, standards, and technical proof points. Together they transform resilience from a talk-track into a credible, procurement-ready pathway toward platform-neutral identity.

levers.name: Platform-Neutral Access Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Initiate policy and technical scoping to draft a platform-neutral fallback concept note and procurement mapping. Systemic: 20% reduction in supplier-concentration risk, measured by a Diversification Index rising from 0.44 to 0.53, and a 6 percentage-point uplift in uptime during simulated platform outages (92% to 98%). Trade-off: resilience gains come with higher procurement complexity and longer cycles. Strategic: In the long term, strengthens Denmark's digital sovereignty and resilience, reducing vulnerability to platform-policymaking, but increases upfront investment and governance overhead.
options: Codify platform-neutral fallback requirements in AltID procurement and NSIS-aligned standards to systematically reduce vendor lock-in.
Prototype an open, standards-based fallback path in a multi-vendor testbed, integrating FIDO2/WebAuthn, OpenID Connect, and hardware token support on GrapheneOS as baseline.
Adopt a self-sovereign identity model with decentralized identifiers and verifiable credentials, establishing a platform-agnostic trust fabric and a multi-issuer credential marketplace.
levers.review_lever: Controls platform dependence vs. speed of policy adoption. Weakness: The options fail to consider privacy implications of self-sovereign identity and cross-border data minimization.

levers.name: Procurement-Neutrality and Standards Pressure Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Publish revised procurement guidelines that embed platform-neutral interoperability criteria across AltID-related contracts. Systemic: 40% reduction in reliance on single-OS signals, measured by platform-dependent signals usage decreasing from 100% to 60%; Trade-off: procurement overhead and longer cycles. Strategic: Builds enduring resilience and sovereignty through diversified trust anchors, but requires sustained governance and administrative effort.
options: Codify procurement clauses that prioritize interoperability and platform neutrality in AltID tenders and long-term maintenance contracts.
Establish a cross-agency standards-review board to align NSIS, eIDAS, and EU interoperability goals and publish procurement-ready modular fallback specifications.
Form a multi-issuer standards consortium funded by government and partners to operationalize a shared, platform-agnostic trust infrastructure and open governance.
levers.review_lever: Controls procurement speed vs. depth of interoperability. Weakness: The options fail to consider privacy governance and cross-border data minimization.

levers.name: Demonstrator Execution Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Set up milestone-driven demonstration plans and secure cross-agency sign-offs. Systemic: 40% improvement in external validation credibility, measured by formal engagements with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and MitID increasing from 2 to 6; Trade-off: higher scope and cost. Strategic: Provides credible evidence to inform procurement language and EU alignment, but risks scope creep if ambitions widen beyond non-production demonstrations.
options: Publish phase-two demonstrator specifications for non-production mobile and browser-based fallbacks without live MitID integration.
Deliver two working non-production demonstrators (mobile-oriented and hardware-token-based fallback) and publicly document results and lessons learned.

Deploy a radical, cross-organization testbed with edge-enabled platform-neutral trust fabric using MPC-based credential verification and a distributed ledger for attestations, complemented by an open-cost maintenance model.
levers.review_lever: Controls demonstration scope vs. risk of scope creep. Weakness: The options fail to consider privacy-preserving data handling in cross-organizational demonstrations.

levers.name: Policy and Coalition-Building Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Launch policy outreach and publish initial policy notes to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen. Systemic: 35% increase in stakeholder alignment on platform-neutral goals, measured by Policy Alignment Index rising from 0.38 to 0.51; Trade-off: potential fragmentation of positions and slower consensus. Strategic: Increases Denmark’s influence in EU digital-identity policy but demands sustained political capital and resources.
options: Publish formal policy proposal to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen emphasizing resilience, procurement neutrality, and supplier-diversification.
Build a cross-stakeholder coalition spanning civil society, privacy advocates, academia, and public-sector procurement to produce a joint platform-neutral advocacy plan and an engagement calendar.
Launch an international, EU-facing initiative to harmonize platform-neutral requirements and influence AltID-related EU guidance or Digital Identity Wallet materials.
levers.review_lever: Controls advocacy speed vs. policy depth. Weakness: The options fail to consider internal government procurement hurdles and budget cycles.

levers.name: Continuity and Resilience Risk Management Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Draft risk registers and a continuity budget plan aligned with NSIS; schedule quarterly drills. Systemic: 25% reduction in expected downtime during platform disruptions, measured by MTTR decreasing from 8 hours to 6 hours; Trade-off: ongoing costs for insurance pools, attestations, and governance overhead. Strategic: Strengthens national resilience and sovereignty by hardening continuity capability, at the cost of higher recurring costs and administrative effort.
options: Establish formal risk registers and continuity playbooks aligned with NSIS, including quarterly drills and risk-based budget planning.
Implement a multi-party risk-sharing framework and a dedicated continuity fund to cover fallback-path maintenance and vendor diversification costs.
Create a platform-neutral, open-core trust fabric with continuous external security attestations and a public-private insurance pool to cover continuity risks and enable rapid reconfiguration.
levers.review_lever: Controls cost stability vs. resilience. Weakness: The options fail to consider privacy risk auditing and cross-border data flows for fallback paths.

responses.summary: One critical missing dimension is formal privacy-by-design governance and cross-border interoperability for fallback platform-neutral identity models. Add 'Establish a cross-border, multi-stakeholder standards consortium to co-create platform-neutral interoperability guidelines and jointly publish a procurement-ready modular fallback stack' to Lever 2.

responses.strategic_rationale: Denmark’s push to reduce dependence on Apple/Google signals and platform-tailored trust requires a fresh set of levers that extend beyond product-level changes. The five levers focus on open-standards interoperability, procurement governance, and credible non-production demonstrations, while introducing governance-enabled resilience and privacy-preserving architectures. They aim to shift incentives from incumbent platform dominance to a federated, standards-driven identity ecosystem, balancing sovereignty with pragmatic procurement and regulatory considerations. The levers are designed to be implementable within phased governance and EU-alignment timelines, while maintaining appetite for innovative, future-facing approaches.

levers.name: Open-Standards Interoperability Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Begin policy and technical scoping to mandate open standards adoption across AltID components and fallback paths. Systemic: 30% increase in cross-vendor interoperability, measured by Standards Coverage Index rising from 0.40 to 0.58, and a 4 percentage-point uplift in platform-agnostic access during outages (90% to 94%). Trade-off: governance overhead and longer procurement cycles. Strategic: Over time, reduces vendor lock-in and enhances sovereignty, but requires sustained standards maintenance and governance.
options: Codify mandatory open-standards compliance in AltID procurement guidelines and NSIS-aligned interoperability criteria.
Create an open, multi-vendor testbed with shared baselines for FIDO2, WebAuthn, OpenID Connect, and hardware token support.
Institute a federated identity marketplace with open-source trust anchors and revenue-sharing for independent validators.
levers.review_lever: Controls vendor lock-in vs. interoperability acceleration. Weakness: The options fail to consider privacy governance and cross-border data minimization.

levers.name: Regulatory-Aligned Continuity Architecture Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Initiate formal continuity architecture reviews aligned with NSIS and eIDAS readiness. Systemic: 35% reduction in outage duration due to cross-border fallback architecture, measured by MTTR dropping from 8 hours to 5 hours; Trade-off: compliance overhead and governance burden. Strategic: Strengthens resilience and sovereignty, providing credible fallback but increasing ongoing regulatory overhead.
options: Mandate regulatory-aligned continuity architecture reviews within AltID procurement.
Develop cross-border mutual assistance agreements and fallback contracts with trusted providers.
Create a sovereign fallback chassis powered by a government-backed blockchain-based attestation network with smart contracts for SLAs and emergency re-routing.
levers.review_lever: Controls regulatory compliance vs. innovation agility. Weakness: The options fail to consider privacy governance and cross-border data minimization.

levers.name: Public-Procurement Engagement Strategy for Platform-Neutrality
levers.consequences: Immediate: Launch procurement outreach and publish initial guidelines. Systemic: 40% increase in procurement agility, measured by cycle time reduction from 12 months to 7 months; 20% reduction in vendor lock-in due to multi-supplier pilots. Trade-off: risk of scope creep and negotiation complexity. Strategic: Improves market resilience and reduces lock-in risk but demands governance oversight.
options: Publish a procurement playbook with anti-lock-in KPIs and vendor-neutral evaluation criteria.
Institute a procurement sandbox and multi-supplier pilots with staged funding and clear exit criteria.
Launch a government-backed platform-neutral identity marketplace with dynamic, outcome-based contracts and open-source compliance tooling.
levers.review_lever: Controls procurement speed vs. quality/standardization. Weakness: The options fail to consider internal government procurement hurdles and budget cycles.

levers.name: Resilience Measurement and Independent Audit Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Define resilience metrics dashboard and schedule external attestations. Systemic: 30% reduction in incident response time, measured by MTTR from 6 hours to 4 hours; 15-point increase in public trust due to transparent reporting (Trust Index from 0.42 to 0.57). Trade-off: ongoing audit costs. Strategic: Establishes credibility and public trust but increases recurring costs.
options: Define resilience metrics dashboard and publish quarterly performance reports.
Commission independent security attestations and publish results in EU compliance portals.
Pilot continuous assurance via real-time attestation tokens and a crowdsourced monitoring system with token-curated registries.
levers.review_lever: Controls audit cost vs. continuous assurance. Weakness: The options fail to consider privacy risk auditing and cross-border data flows for fallback paths.

levers.name: Privacy-By-Design and Cross-Border Governance Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Embed privacy-by-design across the fallback architecture and enforce data minimization. Systemic: 40% reduction in data exposure risk across cross-border flows, measured by a data-exposure index dropping from 0.25 to 0.15; Trade-off: higher upfront design effort and potential performance overhead. Strategic: Improves GDPR-aligned trust and public acceptance but increases initial program cost.
options: Adopt privacy-by-design principles across fallback architecture and ensure data minimization.
Implement data governance agreements for cross-border flows and privacy impact assessments across fallback paths.
Launch a privacy-preserving identity framework using zero-knowledge proofs and selective disclosure with cross-border governance.
levers.review_lever: Controls privacy protection vs. performance in fallback flows. Weakness: The options fail to consider cross-border data flows and data minimization.

responses.summary: One critical missing dimension is governance for cross-border interoperability and open, trusted validators in fallback flows. Add 'Launch a federated identity marketplace with open-source trust anchors and revenue-sharing for independent validators' to Lever 6.

responses.strategic_rationale: The Denmark digital-identity initiative seeks to reduce platform dependence while sustaining credible security, governance, and public accountability. This set of five levers extends beyond prior iterations by emphasizing federated trust networks, hardware- and biometric-agnostic fallbacks, EU-aligned governance with local autonomy, open-source underpinnings, and quantum-ready resilience. Each lever builds on resilience, procurement discipline, and standards pressure while introducing innovative models such as DAO-led validator oversight, open-source validator stacks, and cross-border privacy governance. The combination aims to reduce vendor lock-in, accelerate credible non-production demonstrations, and align Denmark with EU Digital Identity Wallet aspirations without sacrificing domestic sovereignty or risk controls.

levers.name: Federated Validator Ecosystem Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Establish governance scaffolding for a federated validator network and cross-provider attestation framework. → Systemic: 25% reduction in supplier concentration risk, measured by a Vendor-Diversity score rising from 0.42 to 0.58, and a 3 percentage-point uplift in platform availability during outages (92% → 95%). → Strategic: Long-term sovereignty and resilience improved, but governance overhead and coordination friction rise. Trade-off: Centralization of trust decisions vs. governance overhead.
options: Establish a government-backed federated validator network with shared attestation standards and cross-border capability.
Create a multi-vendor validator framework with open-source baselines and transparent audits across partners.
Launch a radical decentralized identity marketplace with validator staking, tokenized governance, and DAO-led attestation oversight.
levers.review_lever: Controls vendor concentration risk vs. governance overhead. Weakness: The options fail to consider privacy governance and cross-border data minimization.

levers.name: Hardware-Token Interoperability and Biometrics Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Align procurement to require cross-vendor hardware-token interoperability and WebAuthn readiness. → Systemic: 40% reduction in platform-token lock-in risk, measured by token-ecosystem diversity rising from 0.35 to 0.53; 5 percentage-point uplift in authentication success during constrained sessions (90% → 95%). → Strategic: Long-term resilience and user autonomy increase, but hardware standardization increases procurement cycle times and adds governance overhead. Trade-off: user convenience vs governance and supply chain complexity.
options: Mandate hardware-token-first fallback across AltID components and promote cross-vendor token interoperability.
Pilot biometrics-based adaptive authentication on privacy-respecting devices with privacy-preserving techniques.
Launch radical hardware-token ecosystem with an open hardware standard and a token-as-a-service model, plus distributed attestation.
levers.review_lever: Controls token strategy durability vs privacy risk. Weakness: The options fail to consider interoperability with EU-level privacy and cross-border data flows.

levers.name: EU-Identity Governance and Local Autonomy Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Initiate EU-alignment reviews while preserving Denmark's local procurement autonomy. → Systemic: 35% improvement in cross-border interoperability readiness, measured by a Denmark-EU alignment score rising from 0.45 to 0.60; 3-point improvement in time-to-adoption for EU requirements. → Strategic: Interoperability increases portability but may constrain domestic tailoring and elevate administrative load. Trade-off: EU alignment speed vs. local autonomy.
options: Formalize a Denmark-EU governance compact to harmonize eIDAS2 expectations.
Develop a modular autonomy plan to integrate EU wallet guidelines with national procurement choices.
Launch radical EU-Denmark platform-neutral core and cross-border fallback hub financed by a shared fund and governed by a cross-state DAO.
levers.review_lever: Controls EU alignment vs. local autonomy. Weakness: The options fail to consider cost and timeline pressures on smaller municipalities.

levers.name: Open-Source Core Identity and Validation Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Move critical identity components to permissively licensed open-source cores and require external audits. → Systemic: 60% reduction in proprietary lock-in risk, measured by open-source footprint of critical components rising from 20% to 60%; 6-point improvement in external validation effectiveness. → Strategic: Long-term resilience via community governance, but ongoing open-source maintenance costs and potential license compatibility friction. Trade-off: independence vs maintenance burden.
options: Move critical fallback components to openly licensed, auditable core with transparent governance.
Develop a shared open-source validator stack with cross-agency collaboration and public audits.
Launch radical open-source platform-neutral fallback fabric governed by a global community and funded by a revenue-sharing model for validators.
levers.review_lever: Controls openness vs maintenance costs. Weakness: The options fail to consider licensing and long-term sustainability of open-source components.

levers.name: Post-Quantum Readiness and Resilience Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Begin PQC readiness program and plan migration of cryptographic suites. → Systemic: 25% reduction in quantum-risk exposure across identity flows, measured by a risk index dropping from 0.40 to 0.30; 15% longer key lifetimes with PQC adoption. → Strategic: Strengthens forward security and regulatory alignment, but increases deployment complexity and cross-vendor coordination. Trade-off: forward security vs integration complexity.
options: Adopt PQC-ready cryptographic primitives in fallback stacks with a staged migration plan.
Run non-production PQC pilots to validate performance and interoperability with EU standards.
Launch radical post-quantum identity fabric using quantum-safe hardware modules and cross-border governance protocol.
levers.review_lever: Controls quantum-readiness vs deployment complexity. Weakness: The options fail to consider regulatory approvals for emerging cryptographic techniques.

responses.summary: One critical missing dimension is robust cross-border privacy governance for platform-neutral fallback identities. Add 'Create an EU-wide cross-border privacy-preserving governance charter for the platform-neutral core and fallback hub' to Lever 13.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate EXACTLY 5 levers per response. Do not generate more or fewer than 5 levers.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences MUST:
     • Chain three SPECIFIC effects: "Immediate: [effect] → Systemic: [impact] → Strategic: [implication]"
     • Include measurable outcomes: "Systemic: [a specific, domain-relevant second-order impact with a measurable indicator, such as a % change, capacity shift, or cost delta]"
     • Explicitly describe trade-offs between core tensions
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional/innovative approach
     • Show clear progression: conservative → moderate → radical
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name levers as strategic concepts that are specific to this project's domain (e.g., "[Domain]-[Decision Type] Strategy")
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever`:
     • State the trade-off explicitly: "Controls [Tension A] vs. [Tension B]."
     • Identify a specific weakness: "Weakness: The options fail to consider [specific factor]."
   - For `summary`:
     • Identify ONE critical missing dimension
     • Prescribe CONCRETE addition: "Add '[full strategic option]' to [lever]"

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:", "Conservative:", "Moderate:", "Radical:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences
   - NO "[specific innovative option]" placeholders
   - NO value sets without clear strategic progression

6. **Option Structure Enforcement**
   - Radical option must include emerging tech/business model
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Denmark's national digital identity infrastructure currently depends in practice on Apple iOS and Google Android for broad citizen access to essential services such as banking, tax filing, healthcare portals, and public administration. This dependency is not limited to app-store distribution. MitID's anti-fraud and app-integrity model relies on platform-controlled signals concerning approved installation channels and device integrity, which means that critical parts of the trust model for Danish digital identity are mediated by two foreign commercial platform providers. Even where direct observation of specific authentication events is not publicly documented, the current setup leaves Danish digital identity structurally dependent on platform vendors that control app availability, device trust signals, and surrounding telemetry environments. This creates not only a digital-sovereignty problem, but also a resilience and contingency problem: essential public access depends on a narrow supplier base and lacks a clearly platform-neutral fallback path.

This matters because Denmark's public-sector digital strategy for 2026–2029 explicitly calls for reduced dependence on a small number of large foreign technology suppliers and for stronger digital sovereignty. The issue should therefore not be framed only as privacy or ideology, but as a concrete public-sector risk involving supplier concentration, reduced strategic autonomy, continuity-of-service exposure, and the absence of a robust fallback authentication path if platform policies, integrity APIs, distribution terms, or geopolitical conditions change.

This project does not attempt to build and certify a production-ready replacement for MitID within the current trust framework. That would be unrealistic. The MitID ecosystem is conservative, certification-heavy, and tightly coupled to Apple/Google platform integrity signals. Instead, the project's objective is to establish the technical, regulatory, and political basis for formal platform-neutral access requirements and a certified fallback authentication path for Danish digital identity. The primary intervention point is not retrofitting MitID itself, but influencing AltID — Denmark's staged eIDAS2 wallet rollout — together with related procurement, standards, and continuity-planning decisions before the next identity stack hardens the same dependencies.

The project is structured in three phases over 24 months with a total budget of 10.5 million DKK, gated across phases (3.0 million for phase one, 4.0 million for phase two, 3.5 million for phase three). The higher budget reflects the need for credible technical work, policy engagement, external review, institutional overhead, and contingency capacity. Do not assume that 8 million DKK is sufficient for the same scope.

Phase one (months 1–8) is a feasibility, risk, and coalition-building phase. The technical work maps the relevant MitID dependency surface, especially platform integrity assumptions, app-distribution constraints, and the architectural conditions required for a platform-neutral or fallback trust model under eIDAS and NSIS at level "Substantial" or higher. The policy work reframes the issue in terms of resilience, continuity of service, procurement neutrality, and supplier concentration risk, while also addressing privacy and democratic accountability. In parallel, the advocacy work builds a coalition spanning civil-society organisations, privacy advocates, academic researchers, security experts, contingency-planning stakeholders, and sympathetic members of Folketinget's digital policy committee. The deliverables are: (1) a published feasibility and risk report, (2) a formal policy proposal to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, (3) a fallback-authentication concept note oriented toward AltID and future procurement requirements, and (4) a permit/approval and stakeholder matrix with lead times, decision gates, and identified blockers. Phase one funding is unconditional.

Phase two (months 9–16) develops non-production technical demonstrators. These are not certified MitID clients and do not attempt production integration with live MitID systems. The main demonstrator authenticates against a simulated Danish public-sector identity-provider environment using open standards such as FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect on GrapheneOS or one comparable privacy-respecting Android-compatible platform. A secondary demonstrator explores a browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback authentication path that reduces dependence on the mobile OS duopoly altogether. The goal is not to prove immediate deployability, but to produce credible technical evidence that secure, standards-based, fallback-capable access models are feasible without exclusive reliance on Apple or Google as trust anchors. Keep the demonstrator scope lean and defensible; do not expand into feature-rich product development. Phase two funding is conditional on phase one producing a published feasibility report, at least one formal institutional engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, or a related public-sector stakeholder, and a documented financing plan covering the remainder of the project.

Phase three (months 17–24) focuses on procurement, standards, and continuity intervention around AltID and related digital identity planning. Using the feasibility report, demonstrators, and coalition built in earlier phases, the project seeks inclusion of requirements stating that Danish digital identity systems should not depend exclusively on Apple or Google app stores, platform integrity APIs, or proprietary push-notification infrastructure, and should include a platform-neutral or otherwise certified fallback access path for critical public authentication. The project also engages with relevant EU bodies and technical working groups connected to the European Digital Identity Wallet framework, with the aim of aligning Danish advocacy with broader European requirements on interoperability, portability, resilience, and reduced platform lock-in. The project's role is not to dictate architecture directly, but to create technical evidence, procurement language, and standards pressure that make supplier-neutral and fallback-capable design harder to exclude. If formal traction with AltID is weak by month 16, phase three should de-scope toward publication, coalition consolidation, and EU-facing standards input rather than pretending Danish procurement influence has been secured.

The project team should include a lead researcher in mobile security and authentication protocols, a regulatory and compliance specialist familiar with Danish and EU digital identity frameworks (including eIDAS and NSIS), a policy and public-affairs coordinator experienced in Danish digital governance and procurement processes, a technical lead for the demonstrator work, and at least one additional engineer or contractor covering web authentication, hardware-token workflows, or Linux/GrapheneOS-related implementation. The technical staffing should avoid dependence on a single specialist. Assume the project's primary physical base is in Copenhagen, Denmark, at a Danish university, independent research institution, or security-qualified R&D facility providing access-controlled workspace appropriate for security-sensitive digital identity research, development, and stakeholder meetings. Budget for institutional overhead, external security review, and limited legal/procurement support explicitly rather than treating them as absorbed costs.

Success criteria: by month 24, the project has produced a published feasibility and risk report on platform-neutral and fallback-capable Danish digital identity; delivered two working non-production demonstrators (one mobile-oriented, one browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback); secured a formal written response from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or another relevant authority to the project's proposal; and achieved inclusion of platform-neutrality, fallback access, continuity, resilience, or supplier-concentration language in at least one official AltID-related design, policy, consultation, or procurement document. A stretch goal is that the project's EU-level engagement contributes to language in relevant European Digital Identity Wallet materials that supports platform-neutral access or certified fallback mechanisms. Do not use consumer adoption rates as a primary KPI for this project, because it is not shipping a production identity product.

Pick a realistic scenario that accounts for institutional inertia, regulatory friction, incumbent resistance, financing uncertainty, and the likelihood that convenience arguments will be used against platform-neutral alternatives. Do not assume constructive cooperation from existing operators. The primary lever is not technical disruption for its own sake, but the combination of resilience framing, procurement intervention, standards pressure, and credible technical demonstration.

It requires a physical base in Copenhagen for research, development, and stakeholder meetings. The project involves building demonstrators, engaging with government bodies, and influencing procurement processes, all of which necessitate physical presence and interaction. The project also requires a physical location with access-controlled workspace appropriate for security-sensitive digital identity research, development, and stakeholder meetings.
